description: Heading-like Title Case documentation.
Tutorial-style documentation that narrates obvious implementation steps.
Package-qualified calls that do not match locally indexed symbols for the imported package.
Same-package calls to symbols not present in the scanned local package context.
Function names that are overly generic without stronger contextual signals.
Very long identifiers with too many descriptive tokens.
Signatures that rely on any or empty interface types.
Secret-like identifiers assigned direct string literals instead of environment lookups.
Tests that assert success expectations without any obvious negative-path signal.
Tests that look skipped, TODO-shaped, or otherwise placeholder-like.
Tests that exercise production code without an obvious assertion or failure signal.
Potentially blocking calls observed between Lock and Unlock.
Likely long-lived goroutines launched after a derived context is created and before the matching cancel call is observed.
Raw go statements launched from inside loops without obvious WaitGroup coordination.
Raw go statements without an obvious context or WaitGroup-like coordination signal.
Looping goroutine literals without an obvious ctx.Done() or done-channel shutdown path.
Repeated Lock or RLock acquisition inside loops.
Struct field tags that repeat the same key more than once.
Struct field tags that do not parse as valid Go tag key/value pairs.
Methods on the same receiver type mix pointer and value receivers.
select { default: ... } inside loops, which often spins instead of blocking.
Functions that already accept context.Context but still create context.Background() or context.TODO() locally.
Derived contexts where deslop cannot find a local cancel() or defer cancel() call.
Standard-library context-aware calls from functions that do not accept context.Context.
Functions that already accept context.Context but still call context-free stdlib APIs like http.Get or exec.Command.
time.Sleep inside loops — often indicates polling or busy-wait style code.
GORM Association(...).Find(...) loaders observed inside loops.
AutoMigrate or schema probes running on request paths instead of startup.
Bun DB handles created directly on request paths instead of reused process-level state.
Bun select-and-scan request paths without a visible limit or pagination marker.
DB pool sizing or lifetime settings changed on request paths.
COUNT(...) or GORM Count(...) observed inside loops.
Request-path GORM flows that run Count(...) and then a broad Find(...) with the same filter shape.
GORM .Create(...) used inside loops with no visible CreateInBatches(...) path in the same function.
DATE/CAST wrapping indexed columns in WHERE clauses, preventing index usage.
database Ping(...) or PingContext(...) called on request paths instead of startup or explicit health checks.
Bulk creates performed with GORM default transaction enabled — SkipDefaultTransaction improves throughput.
GORM Delete(...) chains observed inside loops one row at a time.
Distinct on wide rows without Select projection — a key-only subquery is usually cheaper.
Initial Find followed by a separate Preload query that could be folded into one.
ent clients opened directly inside request handlers instead of shared startup wiring.
Exec(...) or ExecContext(...) used for row-by-row SQL writes inside loops.
COUNT(*) used for an existence check instead of EXISTS or LIMIT 1.
All rows fetched and then sliced in Go instead of using database-level pagination.
Unbounded result sets that could use FindInBatches or cursor iteration.
GORM FirstOrCreate(...) chains observed inside loops.
GORM debug logging enabled on request paths.
Request-path GORM Find(...) chains with no visible Limit(...) step.
GORM chains combining Joins, Preload, and unbounded Find on request paths.
gorm.Open(...) called on request paths instead of process-level setup.
Request-path GORM chains that use Preload(clause.Associations) or other broad preload graphs.
GORM queries on wide models without a Select projection to limit fetched columns.
GORM Session(...) chains allocated inside loops before issuing queries.
LIKE queries with leading wildcards that prevent index usage.
LOWER() or other functions wrapping indexed columns in WHERE clauses, preventing index usage.
Query chains with many OR filter conditions that often scale poorly.
Multiple transactions started on a single request path.
Request-path GORM Find(...) chains that page with Offset(...), which often scales poorly on large lists.
ORDER BY RAND()/RANDOM() observed on request paths.
ORM chains that order results without a Limit on request paths.
pgx.CollectRows used on request paths without a visible LIMIT in the query.
Repeated pgxpool acquire calls inside loops that may churn pooled resources.
pgxpool pools created on request paths instead of reused application-level state.
Per-request pgxpool connectivity probes that add latency outside health-check boundaries.
GORM Preload(...) queries configured and executed inside loops.
Prepare(...) or PrepareContext(...) observed inside loops.
The same literal SQL is prepared multiple times on one request path.
QueryRow(...) or QueryRowContext(...) used inside loops for point lookups that usually want a bulk prefetch path.
GORM Raw(...).Scan(...) chains observed inside loops.
Redis clients created per request instead of reused as shared process infrastructure.
Redis command loops that issue round-trips without pipeline or batch usage.
Per-request Redis ping checks instead of startup or explicit health-probe validation.
The same query template executed multiple times in one function.
Upsert-style writes executed row by row inside loops instead of batched.
New struct allocated for each row scan instead of reusing a scratch variable.
GORM Save used for a single-column update instead of a targeted Update call.
GORM Save(...) writes full models inside loops.
Rows scanned into map[string]any instead of typed structs on hot paths.
ORM lookups (Select, Get, First, etc.) executed inside loops.
database/sql pools opened on request paths instead of process-level setup.
sqlx.Select used on request paths without a visible LIMIT in the query.
Transactions started inside loops instead of once around the wider batch.
IN clauses built from request-driven collections without bound limits.
GORM Update(...), UpdateColumn(...), or Updates(...) calls observed inside loops one row at a time.
GORM Updates(map[string]...) maps allocated inside loops for per-row updates.
Blank identifier assignments that discard an err-like value.
fmt.Errorf calls that reference err without %w.
err != nil branches that jump straight to panic or log.Fatal style exits.
Gin handlers that bind JSON into map[string]any or map[string]interface{} on hot request paths.
Gin handlers that bind query parameters into map[string]any or map[string]interface{} on hot request paths.
Request-path handlers that dump full HTTP requests or responses with httputil.
Same upstream URL called multiple times in one handler.
Environment variable reads observed on request paths instead of cached configuration.
errgroup goroutine fanout without a visible concurrency limit in handlers.
Request-path handlers that read files directly instead of using startup caching or dedicated file-serving paths.
Gin handlers that open uploaded form files and then materialize them with io.ReadAll(...).
Gin handlers that read GetRawData() and later bind JSON from the same request body.
Gin handlers that call c.Copy() once per loop iteration before goroutine fanout.
Verbose body or payload logging observed on likely high-volume Gin routes.
Gzip or zip writers recreated per chunk inside handler loops instead of reusing per stream.
IndentedJSON(...) used on a request path instead of compact JSON rendering.
Handlers that manually marshal JSON and then write it through gin.Context.Data(...).
Export data written in loops without visible buffering in handlers.
Large gin.H payloads built as transient dynamic maps right before JSON rendering.
LoadHTMLGlob or LoadHTMLFiles called on request paths instead of startup initialization.
Database or GORM connections opened inside handlers or middleware instead of process-level setup.
HTTP client allocated inside Gin handlers or middleware instead of being shared.
Regexp compilation inside Gin handlers instead of using precompiled patterns.
Middleware or helper chains that parse the request body after the main handler has already bound it.
Gin handlers that bind the request body multiple times in one function.
Request handlers that drive row-by-row DB writes with no batch path.
Large list or export handlers that materialize everything before writing rather than using chunked or streaming output.
Gin handlers that call ParseMultipartForm(...) with large in-memory thresholds on request paths.
Gin handlers that materialize c.Request.Body with io.ReadAll(...) and then bind the same body again.
Gin handlers that read, rewind, and decode the same request body multiple times.
Gin handlers that call c.JSON(...) or c.PureJSON(...) from inside loops.
Large map-literal response assembly on hot routes where a stable typed response would be cheaper.
Handlers that load files into memory and then write them through gin.Context.Data(...) instead of using file helpers or streaming.
Gin handlers that use ShouldBindBodyWith(...) even though only one body bind is observed.
Template construction or parsing inside Gin handlers instead of startup-time caching.
Upstream HTTP calls made per item inside handler loops.
One upstream HTTP response body decoded into multiple targets in the same handler.
Slice sorting observed inside loops — often cheaper to sort once after the loop.
Slice append followed by reslice each iteration instead of batching.
File or socket reads inside loops without visible bufio buffering.
File or socket writes inside loops without visible bufio buffering.
strings.Builder, bytes.Buffer, or bytes.NewBuffer(...) constructions observed inside loops instead of being reset or reused.
Byte-to-string or string-to-byte conversion observed inside loops in short-lived lookup or append paths.
bytes.Buffer used without Grow when approximate output size is locally visible.
The same byte-slice input is passed through bytes.Split* or bytes.Fields* helpers multiple times in one function.
csv.Writer.Flush() called inside per-row loops, reducing buffering effectiveness.
Same collection traversed multiple times for filter, count, and process steps.
gzip.NewReader(...) or gzip.NewWriter(...) recreated inside iterative paths instead of per stream.
json.NewDecoder(...) constructed repeatedly inside loops instead of reusing a stable decoder per stream.
json.NewEncoder(...) constructed repeatedly inside loops instead of reusing a stable encoder per stream.
make(map[K]V, ...) scratch maps recreated inside loops instead of being reused or prebuilt.
make([]T, ...) scratch slices recreated inside loops instead of being reused.
Map insertions inside loops without a visible size hint on the initial make call.
Append calls inside nested loops without visible preallocation on the outer slice.
Nested-loop lookups or joins that could use a map index for O(1) access.
io.ReadAll(...) materializes a payload and the same binding is then unmarshaled again instead of using a streaming decode path.
regexp.Compile or regexp.MustCompile observed inside obvious iterative paths.
maps.Clone or equivalent map-copy calls observed inside loops.
slices.Clone(...) or similar whole-slice cloning observed inside loops.
Slice append inside a range loop without visible preallocation when the bound is locally known.
slices.Contains(...) or slices.Index(...) used inside loops against a stable-looking slice binding.
Sorting a collection when only the first element or min/max is needed.
Stable value normalization (ToLower, TrimSpace, etc.) repeated inside inner loops.
The same string binding is converted with strconv parsing helpers multiple times in one function.
strings.Builder used without Grow when approximate output size is locally visible.
The same string input is passed through strings.Split* or strings.Fields* helpers multiple times in one function.
html/template or text/template parse calls observed on request-style paths instead of startup-time caching.
time.Parse(...) or ParseInLocation(...) observed inside loops with a stable layout.
url.Parse(...) or ParseRequestURI(...) observed inside loops with a stable-looking base input.
UUID or hash formatting observed inside loops only for log output.
defer statements inside loops that can accumulate resources until function exit.
The same locally created channel appears to be closed more than once in one function body.
File handles opened via os.Open, os.Create, or os.OpenFile without an observed Close() path.
Local http.Client{} literals constructed without an explicit timeout.
HTTP responses acquired locally without an observed resp.Body.Close() call.
Explicit http.Server{} values that omit common timeout fields.
Response decoding or body consumption that happens without an observed StatusCode check.
Handlers that write the response body before calling WriteHeader(...).
init() functions that perform network, file-system, or subprocess side effects.
Package-level variables that are mutated from function bodies instead of kept immutable.
Wrapper structs that mostly forward one-to-one through an interface field with little added policy.
Exported functions or methods that expose raw boolean mode switches in their signatures.
Functions that range over a locally owned channel without an observed close path.
Query result handles that appear locally owned but have no observed rows.Close() call.
A locally owned channel is closed and later used in a send expression.
Repository-local interfaces with one obvious implementation and a very small consumer surface.
Prepared statements or similar DB handles without an observed Close() call.
time.NewTicker(...) is created without an observed Stop() call.
time.After(...) is allocated inside loops instead of reusing a timer or deadline.
Transactions begun and later committed with no observed rollback guard.
`credentials.NewStaticCredentials("AKID...", "secret...", "")` with literal access keys
`session.NewSession()` or `config.LoadDefaultConfig(ctx)` inside handler functions
`cmd.Flags().GetString("flag")` inside `RunE` when the flag could be bound to a variable with `StringVar`
`os.ReadFile("config.json")` or `viper.ReadInConfig()` inside handler or loop code
`dynamodb.Scan` in request handler functions
multiple `os.Getenv` + `strconv.Atoi` / `strconv.ParseBool` chains that could be replaced with a config struct + `envconfig` or `env` library
`log.Error(err); return err` or `logger.Error("failed", zap.Error(err)); return fmt.Errorf("failed: %w", err)` — logging the error then returning it
gRPC handler methods that create new `context.Background()` instead of using the stream/request context
`grpc.Dial(addr, opts...)` or `grpc.NewClient(addr, opts...)` inside handler functions
unary RPC handlers returning or receiving messages > 4MB (inferred from large slice/struct serialization patterns)
`grpc.NewServer()` without keepalive server parameters in long-running services
interceptor/middleware construction inside RPC handler methods instead of at server setup
`zap.S().Debugf("expensive %v", computeDebug())` or `logrus.Debugf("data: %v", expensiveCall())` where the expensive computation happens regardless of log level
`log.Printf` (stdlib) usage in web service handler code
`zap.NewProduction()` or `logrus.New()` inside handler functions
`os.Getenv("KEY")` inside handler functions or loops
`prometheus.NewCounter(prometheus.CounterOpts{...})` inside handler functions
`counter.WithLabelValues(userId)` or `histogram.WithLabelValues(requestPath)` where label values appear to come from user IDs, request paths, or other high-cardinality sources
manual `time.Since(start).Seconds()` + `histogram.Observe(duration)` patterns when `prometheus.NewTimer` would be safer
metrics created with `prometheus.NewCounter`/`NewHistogram` that are never registered with `prometheus.MustRegister` or `promauto`
`redis.NewClient(&redis.Options{...})` inside handler/request functions
multiple sequential `rdb.Get(ctx, key)` or `rdb.Set(ctx, key, val, ...)` calls in the same function without pipeline
`rdb.Keys(ctx, pattern)` in handler or loop code
`rdb.Set(ctx, key, largePayload, ...)` where `largePayload` is the result of `json.Marshal` on a large struct or slice, without visible compression
`rdb.Set(ctx, key, val, 0)` or `rdb.Set(ctx, key, val, redis.KeepTTL)` for cache-like keys without TTL
`s3.GetObject` downloading full objects when only partial data is needed (inferred from subsequent `io.LimitReader` or partial reads)
`s3.ListObjectsV2` without `MaxKeys` or pagination in handler code
`logger.Info(fmt.Sprintf("user %s logged in", user))` instead of structured fields
`viper.GetString("key")` or `viper.GetInt("key")` inside handler functions or loops
The same local JSON payload binding is unmarshaled into multiple targets in one function.
The same local protobuf payload binding is unmarshaled into multiple targets in one function.
The same local XML payload binding is unmarshaled into multiple targets in one function.
The same local YAML payload binding is unmarshaled into multiple targets in one function.
Obvious make, new, or buffer-construction calls inside loops.
`binary.Read(r, order, &singleField)` for reading a single integer
`bufio.NewScanner(r)` without `scanner.Buffer()` when processing files with lines > 64KB
`for k := range m { delete(m, k) }` in Go 1.21+ codebases
`dst = append(dst, src...)` when `dst` is known empty and `len(src)` is known
`csv.NewReader(r)` without `ReuseRecord = true` when records are processed one at a time and not stored
`defer` statements inside loops with > 100 iterations or visible hot-path markers
exported functions with `any` or `interface{}` parameters when concrete types would suffice
`if err.Error() == "some error"` string comparison for error checking
`errors.New("some error")` called repeatedly in hot paths instead of a package-level sentinel
`fmt.Errorf("context: %v", err)` instead of `%w`
fmt formatting calls such as Sprintf inside loops.
Calls that load an entire payload into memory instead of streaming.
`go func() { result <- compute() }()` followed by `<-result` where the goroutine is immediately awaited
`io.ReadAll(req.Body)` in HTTP handlers without `io.LimitReader`
`[]interface{}` or `[]any` used to pass homogeneous typed data
`ioutil.ReadAll` usage when `io.ReadAll` is available (Go 1.16+)
`data, _ := json.Marshal(v); w.Write(data)` when `json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(v)` would stream directly
`json.Unmarshal` into `map[string]any` for numeric data without `UseNumber()`
`len(s) == 0` used interchangeably with `s == ""`
Opt-in deeper semantic signal for allocations that also sit inside nested loop structure.
Opt-in deeper semantic signal for repeated string concatenation inside nested loops without obvious builder usage.
Query shapes like leading-wildcard LIKE or ORDER BY without LIMIT that often scale poorly.
`for k := range m { delete(m, k) }` patterns
`if _, ok := m[k]; ok { v := m[k] }` — two map lookups for the same key
`m[k] = append(m[k], v)` in loops without pre-allocating inner slices
`func (s MyStruct) Method()` where `MyStruct` contains a `sync.Mutex` or `sync.RWMutex` field
Database-style query calls issued inside loops. The opt-in semantic pack can raise severity when nested loops also appear.
`panic()` used for expected error conditions like invalid input or missing config
`for _, v := range largeStructSlice` where the struct is > 64 bytes
`for i := 0; i < len(s); i++ { c := s[i] }` on strings that should iterate runes
reflect package calls inside loops.
encoding/json.Marshal or MarshalIndent inside loops — repeated allocation and serialization hot spots.
chains like `strings.TrimSpace(strings.ToLower(strings.TrimPrefix(s, ...)))` that scan the string multiple times
`select { case v := <-ch: ... }` with only one case and no default
`var result []T` followed by `append` in a loop where the iteration count is visible from a `len()` or range source
`sort.Sort(sort.StringSlice(s))` or custom `sort.Interface` implementations for basic types
`fmt.Sprintf("%d", n)` where `n` is clearly an integer type
`fmt.Sprintf("%s:%s", a, b)` where only `%s` verbs are used
`builder.WriteString(a + b)` where `a` and `b` are separate bindings
Repeated string concatenation inside loops (O(n^2) risk).
`dir + "/" + file` or manual path assembly via `+` concatenation
`fmt.Errorf("failed: %s", err.Error())` where `%s` on `err.Error()` is used instead of `%w` on `err`
`[]byte(s)[0]` or `string(b) == "x"` for single-character comparisons
`strings.Index(s, sub) != -1` or `strings.Index(s, sub) >= 0` patterns
`if strings.HasPrefix(s, p) { s = strings.TrimPrefix(s, p) }`
`if strings.HasSuffix(s, p) { s = strings.TrimSuffix(s, p) }`
`strings.ReplaceAll(s, "x", "y")` where both old and new are single characters
`mu.Lock(); count++; mu.Unlock()` for simple integer counters
`mu.RLock(); v := config.X; mu.RUnlock()` for read-mostly config that changes rarely
repeated `make([]byte, size)` or `new(T)` in hot paths where the object is short-lived and could be pooled
`sub := original[a:b]` followed by `sub = append(sub, ...)` with no capacity bound
`time.Now()` called on every iteration of a tight inner loop
`v := i.(T)` without the comma-ok form in non-panic-safe code
multiple sequential `if _, ok := i.(T1); ok { ... } else if _, ok := i.(T2); ok { ... }` patterns
unbuffered channels `make(chan T)` when the number of producers/messages is known at construction time
`map[int]bool` or `map[int]struct{}` used as a set for small dense integer ranges
`copy := append([]T(nil), original...)` when `copy` is only read, never mutated
`for { wg.Add(1); go func() { ... wg.Done() }() }` where `wg.Add` could be called once before the loop with the count
Literal SELECT * query shapes.
`xml.NewDecoder(r)` without setting `Strict = false` when processing trusted XML
`bcrypt.GenerateFromPassword(pw, cost)` where `cost` is literally `< 10` or `bcrypt.MinCost`
`C.CString(goString)` without a corresponding `C.free` in the same function, or deferred `C.free`
`[]byte("...")` used directly as arguments to `cipher.NewGCM`, `aes.NewCipher`, or similar encryption constructor calls
constant or zero-valued byte slices used as IV/nonce arguments to `cipher.NewCBCEncrypter`, `gcm.Seal`, or similar
`http.Cookie{...}` for session/auth cookies without `HttpOnly: true`
`http.Cookie{...}` without `SameSite` set, particularly for auth/session cookies
`http.Cookie{...}` literals without `Secure: true` for session or authentication cookies
`Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` combined with `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`, or CORS middleware configured with `AllowAllOrigins: true` in Gin/Echo/Chi
`net/http/pprof` import or `http.Handle("/debug/pprof/", ...)` registration without access control
`net.LookupHost` or `net.LookupAddr` results used in access control decisions
direct use of `cipher.Block.Encrypt` / `cipher.Block.Decrypt` without a block mode wrapper (CBC, CTR, GCM)
`fmt.Errorf("... %s", os.Getenv("SECRET_KEY"))` or similar patterns that embed environment variable values in errors
`c.JSON(500, gin.H{"error": err.Error()})` or `http.Error(w, err.Error(), 500)` returning internal error details to the client
`filepath.Join(baseDir, userInput)` without subsequent `filepath.Rel` or path-containment validation
`fmt.Sprintf("%+v", user)` or `fmt.Printf("%v", config)` on structs that contain password/secret/token fields
`math/rand.Intn()`, `rand.Float64()`, etc. (global source) in hot handler or goroutine paths
`for _, v := range items { go func() { use(v) }() }` without rebinding `v` inside the loop body (pre-Go 1.22)
`grpc.Dial(addr, grpc.WithInsecure())` or `grpc.WithTransportCredentials(insecure.NewCredentials())` in non-test code
`tls.Config{MinVersion: tls.VersionTLS10}` or `tls.VersionTLS11` or `tls.VersionSSL30`
`tls.Config{InsecureSkipVerify: true}` in non-test code
`w.Header().Set(name, userInput)` or `w.Header().Add(name, userInput)` where the value contains unvalidated user input that could contain `\r\n`
HTTP handler functions that write responses without setting `X-Content-Type-Options`, `X-Frame-Options`, or `Content-Security-Policy` headers (or without security header middleware)
POST/PUT/DELETE handler registration without evidence of CSRF token middleware
`http.ListenAndServe` (non-TLS) usage in production-like code (not test files, not localhost bindings)
`math/rand` usage (any of `rand.Int`, `rand.Intn`, `rand.Read`, `rand.New`) in functions whose names suggest security use (token generation, key generation, password, nonce, salt, session)
JWT verification code that accepts `"none"` or `alg: ""` as valid signing methods, or uses `jwt.Parse` without `WithValidMethods`
`jwt.NewWithClaims(jwt.SigningMethodHS256, claims).SignedString([]byte("hardcoded"))` where the signing key is a string literal
string concatenation or `fmt.Sprintf` building LDAP filter strings with user input
login/authentication handler functions (name contains `Login`, `Authenticate`, `SignIn`) that don't reference rate limiting, throttling, or brute-force protection mechanisms
`exec.Command(userInput)` or `exec.Command("sh", "-c", variable)` where the command string appears to come from a function parameter or request binding
`recover()` in HTTP middleware that sends the panic message/stack to the response writer
struct fields named `Password`, `Passwd`, or `Pwd` stored as `string` in database model structs without evidence of hashing
map reads/writes from multiple goroutines without mutex or `sync.Map` protection (detect goroutine launches + shared map access patterns)
`rsa.GenerateKey(rand, bits)` where `bits` is literally `< 2048`
`log.Printf`, `slog.Info`, `zap.String`, `logrus.WithField` calls that include variables named `password`, `secret`, `token`, `apiKey`, `creditCard`, `ssn`, or similar
goroutines appending to a shared slice without synchronization
`smtp.PlainAuth` used without TLS (`smtp.SendMail` to non-TLS endpoints)
Query execution calls where SQL is constructed dynamically with concatenation or fmt.Sprintf.
`ssh.ClientConfig{HostKeyCallback: ssh.InsecureIgnoreHostKey()}` in non-test code
`http.Get(userInput)` or `http.NewRequest("GET", userInput, nil)` where the URL comes from request parameters
exported struct fields containing sensitive data (Password, Secret, Token, APIKey, PrivateKey) without `json:"-"` tags in API response structs
`os.Create("/tmp/myapp-data.txt")` or `os.OpenFile("/tmp/" + fixedName, ...)` with predictable filenames
`template.HTML(userInput)` or `template.JS(userInput)` type conversions on data from request parameters
`text/template` used to generate HTML content (check for HTML tags in template literals or `.html` file extensions in `ParseFiles`)
`token == expectedToken` or `bytes.Equal(token, expected)` for comparing authentication tokens, API keys, or HMAC values
`os.Stat(path)` or file existence check followed by `os.Open(path)` or `os.Create(path)` without atomic operations
`unsafe.Pointer` casts between incompatible types, particularly `uintptr` arithmetic followed by cast back to `unsafe.Pointer`
`http.Redirect(w, r, r.FormValue("redirect_url"), 302)` or `c.Redirect(302, c.Query("url"))` without URL validation
Direct use of weak standard-library crypto packages such as crypto/md5, crypto/sha1, crypto/des, and crypto/rc4.
`md5.New()`, `sha1.New()`, `md5.Sum()`, `sha1.Sum()` used for integrity checks, checksums, or MAC operations (not just `weak_crypto` import-level detection)
`websocket.Upgrader{CheckOrigin: func(r *http.Request) bool { return true }}` or missing `CheckOrigin`
`os.OpenFile(path, flag, 0666)` or `os.WriteFile(path, data, 0777)` with world-readable/writable permissions
`xml.NewDecoder(r)` processing untrusted XML without setting `d.Entity = nil` and without input size limits
`yaml.Unmarshal(untrustedInput, &target)` using `gopkg.in/yaml.v2` without size limits
Directories that mix base Go package names after ignoring the _test suffix.
Import blocks that place stdlib imports after third-party imports.
Unusually enthusiastic or emoji-heavy production comments.
File mixes snake_case and camelCase function naming conventions.
Comments that narrate obvious implementation steps instead of explaining intent.
Very small helper functions that have unusually long, textbook-style docstrings.
Heavy ecosystem imports with little local evidence of real need.
Highly similar non-test function bodies repeated across multiple Python files.
Project repeats the same long string literal across multiple files.
Repeated exception-handling block shapes in one file.
Repository repeats the same SQL-like query fragment across multiple files.
Highly similar utility logic shared between test and production code.
Repository repeats the same data transformation pipeline stages across multiple functions.
Repeated validation guard pipelines across functions in one file.
Project repeats the same long string literal multiple times in one file.
Celery tasks dispatch .delay(...) or .apply_async(...) inside loops without an obvious canvas primitive such as group() or chord().
Celery tasks synchronously wait on AsyncResult.get(...) instead of handing work off asynchronously.
Celery task bodies repeatedly read environment configuration instead of using startup-time bootstrap.
click or typer commands parse config files on each invocation instead of using shared bootstrap or dependency setup.
click or typer commands scatter repeated environment lookups through the command body.
click or typer commands allocate HTTP clients inside command bodies instead of reusing a shared client factory.
Django views call .all() without visible pagination, slicing, or limiting.
Django code creates one model at a time inside loops instead of using bulk_create().
Django code deletes individual models inside loops instead of using set-based queryset deletion.
Django views or request paths reference schema or migration operations that belong in migrations.
Django queryset iteration shows N+1 risk with no visible select_related(...) or prefetch_related(...).
Django querysets use count() for existence checks instead of exists().
The same Django queryset appears to be evaluated multiple times in one function.
len(queryset) is used where queryset.count() would avoid loading every row.
Django querysets use order_by(\"?\") or equivalent random ordering on request paths.
Django request or service code executes raw SQL inside loops instead of batching.
Django code saves full model instances in loops without update_fields or bulk updates.
Django code updates one row at a time inside loops instead of using bulk or set-based updates.
Django loops hydrate full model instances where values(), values_list(), or only() would likely be cheaper.
FastAPI background task dispatches appear to rely on default exception behavior without visible error handling.
FastAPI dependencies construct HTTP clients per request instead of using app lifespan or shared setup.
FastAPI response models use ORM conversion paths without visible ORM compatibility configuration.
FastAPI sync route handlers perform blocking I/O instead of using async routes or executor offload.
Flask views repeatedly read app.config on request paths instead of consuming bootstrapped settings.
Flask code enables debug=True in application runtime paths.
